title: Information Asymmetry, Mispricing, and Security Issuance
abstract: I examine the effects of information asymmetry–driven mispricing on security issuance. Using predisclosure changes in purchase obligations as a proxy for information asymmetry–driven mispricing, I find that managers avoid (prefer) issuing securities when they perceive their firms to be undervalued (overvalued). The effects of information asymmetry–driven mispricing are stronger on equity issuance than debt issuance. Consequently, undervaluation (overvaluation) causes an increase (decrease) in leverage. These effects are more pronounced for firms, periods, and securities associated with greater information asymmetry. The stock-trading patterns that managers follow suggest that their perceived mispricing is an important factor in both private and firm-level decisions.

article.introduction:
A manager typically has superiorinformation about his or her firm's future performance relative to what outside investors know. This information asymmetry can cause a firm to be undervalued or overvalued in the market relative to the manager's perception. Such information asymmetry–driven mispricing may affect a range of managerial decisions. In this paper, I investigate the effect of information asymmetry–driven mispricing on security issuance.
Addressing this question presents a challenge because it is difficult to measure mispricing caused by information asymmetry. First, measuring mispricing, regardless of the cause, is difficult because the true value of firm shares is not observable. Some studies on market timing use the market-to-book ratio as a proxy for mispricing (Baker and Wurgler (2002), DeAngelo, DeAngelo, and Stulz (2010)). A key drawback of this method is that the market-to-book ratio can also proxy for growth options or time-varying adverse selection costs. Other studies interpret preissuance share price run-ups and postissuance underperformance as evidence of market timing (Loughran and Ritter (1995,1997), DeAngelo, DeAngelo, and Stulz (2010)). However, these findings can be explained by Q-theory (Li, Livdan, and Zhang (2009)) or rational theory in a real options framework (Carlson, Fisher, and Giammarino (2006)). Several studies use an earnings-based valuation model to estimate the mispricing of a share relative to its economic value (D'Mello and Shroff (2000), Rhodes-Kropf, Robinson, and Viswanathan (2005), Elliott, Koeter-Kant, and Warr (2008)), but these studies assume that managers forecast future earnings perfectly, which is unrealistic. More importantly, the methods above do not allow us to determine whether the mispricing is caused by investor irrationality or information asymmetry.
To overcome this challenge, I exploit the mandatory disclosure of purchase obligations (POs) in 10-K filings.1The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) defines a PO as an enforceable and legally binding agreement to purchase goods or services in the future. A PO thus represents a quantity of production inputs that a firm has committed to purchase in the near future. A PO likely increases (decreases) when a manager expects demand for the firm's products to increase (decrease). A PO can therefore proxy for a manager's expectations regarding the firm's future performance. Furthermore, although POs are made throughout the year, only snapshots of POs are disclosed in 10-Ks at the end of each year, that is, outside investors do not receive information on POs until they are disclosed in 10-K filings. As such, POs proxy for a manager's forecasts of the firm's future performance but remain unknown to investors over the course of a given year. In this paper, I exploit predisclosure changes in the POs of manufacturing firms as a proxy for mispricing relative to a manager's private information about firm value to examine the effect of information asymmetry–driven mispricing on security issuance.
Two conditions need to be satisfied to validate the use of predisclosure changes in POs as a proxy for information asymmetry–driven mispricing. First, predisclosure changes in POs should predict future changes in security prices. If predisclosure changes in POs are not positively related to future changes in security prices, the assumption that POs represent a manager's superior information about firm value cannot be satisfied. Second, predisclosure changes in POs should be private information. If outside investors have the same updated information as managers throughout the year, predisclosure changes in POs would not predict mispricing.
To verify the first condition, I examine whether changes in POs predict changes in future stock prices. To this end, I defineΔPOtasPOtminusPOt−1, wherePOtis the total dollar amount of POs recorded in 10-Ks in fiscal yeartscaled by total assets. Similarly, I defineΔPOtt+1asPOtt+1minusPOt−1t, wherePOtt+1is the dollar amount of POs recorded in 10-Ks in fiscal yeartthat are due during yeart+1 scaled by total assets, and I defineΔPOtrestasPOtrestminusPOt−1rest, wherePOtrestisPOtminusPOtt+1. I find thatΔPOt(ΔPOtt+1) is positively associated with size and book-to-market (BM)-adjusted buy-and-hold returns duringt+1 (BHARt+1), which are computed by adding daily abnormal returns for all trading days during fiscal yeart+1. In particular, a one-standard-deviation increase inΔPOt(ΔPOtt+1) is associated with an increase inBHARt+1of approximately 1.8 (2.7) percentage points, which accounts for approximately 5.1% (7.7%) of the standard deviation ofBHARt+1. The estimate also implies that an increase inΔPOt(ΔPOtt+1) from the 25th percentile to the 75th percentile increasesBHARt+1by 0.3 (0.6) percentage points.2
To verify the second condition that predisclosed POs constitute private information I investigate stock price responses following the disclosure of POs. If predisclosure POs constitute private information, stock prices should respond immediately in an appropriate direction following the disclosure of 10-Ks. The results confirm this expectation. A one-standard-deviation increase inΔPOt(ΔPOtt+1) is associated with an increase inBHAR(−1,1) of approximately 28 (33) bps, which accounts for 7.6% (8.9%) of its standard deviation.3The estimate also implies that an increase inΔPOt(ΔPOtt+1) from the 25th percentile to the 75th percentile increasesBHAR(−1,1) by 4 (8) bps. Taken together, these two sets of results support the use of predisclosure changes in POs as a proxy for information asymmetry–driven mispricing.4
I next turn to the effects of information asymmetry–driven mispricing on securities issuance. If a manager has private information that the firm's value will increase in the near future, that is, that the firm is currently undervalued, then the manager is likely to avoid issuing securities and may even pursue stock repurchases or debt retirements.5Similarly, if a manager's private information indicates that the firm's value will decrease in the future, that is, that the firm is currently overvalued, then their incentives to issue securities strengthen.6If the value of debt is less sensitive to information than that of equity, however, the effects of information asymmetry–driven mispricing on net debt issues would be smaller than those on net equity issues. Consequently, a firm's financial leverage would increase (decrease) when it is undervalued (overvalued) based on a manager's perception. Because increases (decreases) in POs indicate undervaluation (overvaluation), changes in POs are expected to be negatively associated with contemporaneous net security issuance, especially net equity issuance, but positively associated with financial leverage.
My empirical results support this logic. Controlling for firm characteristics, firm fixed effects and industry-year fixed effects,7a one-standard-deviation decrease inΔPOt(ΔPOtt+1) is associated with an increase in contemporaneous net equity issuance of 1.1% (1.8%) of total securities outstanding, which accounts for approximately 8.7% (13.8%) of the sample standard deviation of net equity issuance. The estimate also implies that a decrease inΔPOt(ΔPOtt+1) from the 75th percentile to the 25th percentile increases equity issuance by 0.2% (0.4%) of total securities outstanding. On the other hand, a one-standard-deviation decrease inΔPOt(ΔPOtt+1) is associated with an increase in contemporaneous net debt issuance of 0.23% (0.35%) of total securities outstanding, which accounts for approximately 2.3% (3.5%) of the sample standard deviation of net debt issuance. Another economic implication of this result is that a decrease inΔPOt(ΔPOtt+1) from the 75th percentile to the 25th percentile is expected to increase debt issuance by 0.03% (0.08%) of total securities outstanding. Thus, the effect ofΔPOton net equity issuance is significantly stronger than that on net debt issuance, which implies thatΔPOtis positively associated with contemporaneous financial leverage.
I provide empirical evidence that supports the interpretation that the negative association betweenΔPOtand security issuance is due to information asymmetry. I first exploit cross-sectional variation in the degree of information asymmetry and show that the negative association betweenΔPOtand issuance is stronger among firms with more severe information asymmetry problems. For example, I do not find a significant association betweenΔPOtand contemporaneous security issuance among firms that regularly report POs on their 10-Qs, which are expected to suffer less severe information asymmetry about POs compared with firms that report POs only on annual reports. In addition, using frequently used proxies for the level of information asymmetry, such as analyst coverage, firm age, and R&D intensity, I find the negative association betweenΔPOtand contemporaneous security issuance to be stronger among high-information-asymmetry firms. Next, I exploit time-varying information asymmetry. I find that the negative association betweenΔPOtand quarterly net security issuance is strongest in the last quarter of yeart, when information asymmetry aboutΔPOtis expected to be most severe. In addition, I find no association betweenΔPOtand net security issuance during the first quarter oft+1, indicating that the negative association disappears following disclosure of POs. Furthermore, I exploit variation in the degree of information sensitivity between public debt and private debt. I find that the negative association betweenΔPOtand net debt issuance is due to public debt, which is more information sensitive.
A potential concern with the analysis above is that the negative association betweenΔPOtand contemporaneous security issuance is due to time-varying fundamentals that are correlated with POs. For example, a decrease (increase) in POs may be associated with increased (decreased) demand for external financing. However, I do not find any association betweenΔPOtand contemporaneous or future financing deficit.
Lastly, managers' stock trading and option exercise patterns are consistent with the view that they believe changes in POs predict mispricing. I find that managers engage in significantly more stock sales (purchases) when predisclosed POs predict overvaluation (undervaluation) of shares. Similarly, managers' option exercises are negatively associated withΔPOt. These results suggest that managers' perceived mispricing is an important determinant of both private and firm-level decisions.
In addition to contributing to the literature on market timing, as explained above, this study contributes to studies on information asymmetry and capital structure. The pecking-order theory of Myers (1984) and Myers and Majluf (1984) implies that firms should prefer debt to equity because equity involves a higher level of information asymmetry than debt. Consistent with this prediction, Bharath, Pasquariello, and Wu (2009) find that firms with greater information asymmetry fund a greater percentage of their financing deficit with debt. Although both Bharath, Pasquariello, and Wu (2009) and my study examine information asymmetry and security issuance, Bharath, Pasquariello, and Wu's (2009) measure of information asymmetry is based on the assessment of adverse-selection risk in the secondary market between informed and uninformed investors, whereas my measure is based directly on the private information held by managers and thus identifies information asymmetry between insiders and outsiders more cleanly. More importantly, Bharath, Pasquariello, and Wu (2009) do not consider the sign of mispricing, that is, whether information asymmetry causes underpricing or overpricing of firm shares, while my findings indicate that the sign of mispricing plays an important role in capital structure.
Korajczyk, Lucas, and McDonald (1991,1992) consider time-varying information asymmetry. Korajczyk, Lucas, and McDonald (1991) use earnings announcements as information events and show that firms tend to issue equity following earnings announcements, especially those with positive stock price responses.8More importantly, they show that the price drop on the date of an equity issuance announcement increases since the last information release. Kerr and Ozel (2015) extend Korajczyk, Lucas, and McDonald (1991) by analyzing both debt and equity issuance and find that clustering of issuance after earnings announcements increases with a security's sensitivity to information asymmetry. Unlike Korajczyk, Lucas, and McDonald (1991), however, Kerr and Ozel (2015) find that the fraction of bond offerings and seasoned equity offerings (SEOs) made after earnings announcements that reveal good news is largely statistically indistinguishable from those that reveal bad news. They attribute this result to firms' desire to avoid the potential costs associated with the omission of negative material information. Although these studies and my paper investigate time-varying information asymmetry and security issues, there is an important difference. In particular, these studies do not directly measure managers' private information but instead show evidence consistent with information asymmetry increasing since the previous information releases, whereas I measure managers' private information based on POs and thus am able to provide more direct evidence on the implications of information asymmetry.
This paper is also related to Lee (2010), who empirically shows that, during the 2004 to 2007 period, firms entered into additional POs when they expected demand for their projects to increase. In particular, he finds that growth in POs is positively associated with future sales, earnings, and abnormal stock returns. Unlike what I find, however, he finds that the market fails to fully incorporate the implications of POs, as announcement returns are not significantly associated with changes in POs, resulting in longer delays in stock price responses than I observe in my sample firms.9
This paper also engages with the insider trading literature. A prevalent finding of this literature is that insiders use the private information they possess at the time they trade. Several studies identify particular types of private information that managers use, including foreknowledge of future earnings (Ke, Huddart, and Petroni (2003)), disclosure of material corporate events such as bankruptcies (Seyhun and Bradley (1997)), dividend initiations (John and Lang (1991)), SEOs (Karpoff and Lee (1991)), and stock repurchases (Lee, Mikkelson, and Partch (1992)). I contribute to this literature by adding the information reflected in POs as a specific type of private information that managers use to their advantage. Compared with trades executed prior to earnings announcements or disclosures of material corporate events, trades based on predisclosed POs may be less subject to legal jeopardy and as a result could be more prevalent. This paper is also related to Jenter (2005), who shows that managers' private trading and firm-level security issuance are closely related. My study differs from Jenter (2005) as I link private trades and firm-level security issues through a particular source of private information that managers possess. In contrast, Jenter (2005) suggests that insiders may not use valid private information.
The remainder of this paper proceeds as follows. In SectionI, I introduce the baseline empirical strategy and describe the data. I validate the use of predisclosure changes in POs as a proxy for information asymmetry–driven mispricing in SectionII. In SectionIII, I present results of the baseline tests. SectionIVpresents results of tests that exploit variation in the levels of information asymmetry. I address alternative explanations in SectionV. SectionVIpresents evidence that managers believe changes in POs predict mispricing of shares. I conclude in SectionVII.
article.I. Baseline Strategy and Data:
In January 2003, the SEC issued a final rule to implement Section 401(a) of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002, which requires public firms to disclose overviews of their off-balance-sheet arrangements in their 10-Ks for fiscal years ending on or after December 15, 2003. The rule requires companies to disclose an overview of contractual obligations in tabular format in their annual reports (typically Form 10-K). POs are among the four categories of contractual obligations affected, along with long-term debt obligations, capital lease obligations, and operating lease obligations. The SEC defines a PO as an enforceable and legally binding agreement to purchase goods or services in the future and requires firms to disclose both the total amount of POs and the approximate timing of payments due in future periods. POs thus indicate the quantity of production inputs a firm has committed to purchase.
POs likely increase (decrease) when a manager expects demand for the firm's products to increase (decrease). POs can therefore be regarded as reflecting the manager's expectations about the firm's future performance. Furthermore, while POs are made throughout a year, only snapshots of them are disclosed in 10-Ks at each year-end and thus outside investors do not have this information during the fiscal year. As such, POs proxy for a manager's forecasts of a firm's future performance but remain unknown to investors throughout a given year.
My empirical strategy is to use predisclosed changes in POs as a proxy for information asymmetry–driven mispricing to examine the effects of mispricing on security issuance and capital structure. An increase in POs would indicate that a manager holds private information suggesting that the performance and value of the firm will increase, and thus that the firm is undervalued in the current market. In contrast, a decrease in POs would indicate that the manager holds private information suggesting that the firm's future performance and value will decline, and thus that the firm is overvalued in the current market. For this strategy to work, however, two conditions must be satisfied. First, predisclosure changes in POs should predict future changes in security prices. Second, outside investors should not have the information included in such POs before disclosure. Below I begin by ensuring that these two conditions are satisfied. I then examine the effects of changes in POs on contemporaneous security issuance and financial leverage.
I hand-collect information on POs from 10-K filings for the period 2003 to 2015. I consider a sample of public manufacturing firms (SICs 2000 to 3999) in the Compustat universe. I separately record information on the total dollar amount of POs and POs due in the following year. If a firm does not report POs, I record them as zero. I exclude firms that never report POs during the sample period. Other financial data come from Compustat and stock return data from the Center for Research in Security Prices (CRSP). The sample of seasoned common equity offerings and stock repurchases is obtained from Thomson Financial's Securities Data Corporation (SDC) database. Analysts' earnings forecasts, actual realized earnings, and analyst coverage are taken from Institutional Brokers' Estimates System International, Inc. (I/B/E/S). Data on managers' trading activities are collected from Form 4 reported to the SEC, and data on public debt are obtained from Capital IQ. Lastly, data on managers' option exercises and holdings come from Execucomp.
I defineDebtas total debt (Compustat item DLC plus item DLTT), andEquityas the market value of shareholders' equity (CSHO×PRCC_F). Following a number of studies, I defineEquityIssuesas the total amount of funds received from issuance of common and preferred stock (Compustat item SSTK), andEquityRepurchasesas the total amount of funds used to repurchase common and preferred stock (PRSTKC) (Baker and Wurgler (2002), Chang, Dasgupta, and Hillary (2006), Kisgen (2006), Butler et al. (2011), Malmendier, Tate, and Yan (2011), Kahle and Stulz (2013), Balakrishnan et al. (2014), Leary and Roberts (2014)). I defineDebtIssuesas long-term debt issuance (DLTIS), andDebtRetirementsas long-term debt reduction (DLTR).
I defineEquityIssueDtas an indicator variable that is equal to one if a firm issues equity valued at more than 1% of the lagged value of total securities, and zero otherwise. Similarly, I defineRepurchaseDtas an indicator variable that is equal to one if a firm repurchases equity valued at more than 1% of the lagged value of total securities, and zero otherwise. I defineDebtIssueDtas an indicator variable equal to one if a firm issues debt valued at more than 1% of the lagged value of total securities, and zero otherwise. Similarly, I defineDebtReductionDtas an indicator variable equal to one if a firm reduces debt valued at more than 1% of the lagged value of total securities, and zero otherwise.
Following Welch (2011), to remove the effects of nonfinancial liabilities, I define leverage (Leverage) as debt over the sum of debt and the book value of equity. I define investment (Investment) as capital expenditures, scaled by lagged property, plant, and equipment. Tobin's Q (Tobin'sQ) is defined as total assets minus common equity plus the market value of equity, scaled by total assets. Firm size (Size) is the natural logarithm of total assets and cash flow (Cashflow) is earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization (EBITDA) scaled by lagged total assets. Cash holdings (Cash) are cash and cash equivalents, scaled by total assets, and tangibility (Tangibility) is defined as property, plant, and equipment, scaled by total assets. I defineBHAR(−1,1) as size and BM–adjusted buy-and-hold returns during a three-day period starting one day before the 10-K filing date. In particular, I use size and BM-matched portfolios as benchmarks to compute abnormal stock price performance. In constructing benchmark portfolios, I first sort firms into quintiles based on the 20th, 40th, 60th, and 80th percentiles of the market value of shareholders' equity. I then sort each size quintile portfolio into five equal BM quintiles. I defineBHARtas size and BM–adjusted buy-and-hold returns during fiscal yeart. In particular, I add daily abnormal returns of all trading days duringt. All other variables are defined in AppendixAand winsorized at 1% in both tails of the distribution.
TableIpresents summary statistics. Approximately 95% of the firm-year observations show positive POs outstanding. On average, sample firms have POs of approximately 9.0% of total assets, most of which are due in the following year. An averageNetEquityIssueis 0.017 andNetDebtIssueis 0.011. In addition, 13.5% of sample firms issue equity, while 18.3% repurchase equity, 20.3% issue debt, and 19.1% reduce debt. Sample firms' averageLeverageis 0.283, and on average sample firms make investments of approximately 33.9% of the value of property, plant, and equipment.
Regarding variables that capture managers' trading activities, I defineNetSelltas the number of shares sold minus the number of shares purchased, scaled by the lagged total number of shares outstanding, a definition that is similar to that in Sias and Whidbee (2010).10Because the range of the variable resulting from this scaling is a small interval around zero, I multiply the variable by 1,000. The average ofNetSelltis 0.234, indicating that transactions executed by managers are predominantly sales. I defineSelltas the number of shares sold times 1,000, andBuytas the number of shares purchased times 1,000, both scaled by the lagged total number of shares outstanding. In addition, I defineSell/(Sell+Buy)tas the number of shares sold over the sum of the number of shares purchased and the number of shares sold, following Piotroski and Roulstone (2005). I setSell/(Sell+Buy)tto missing in years in which the manager does not trade shares. Next, I computeNetSelltFirm,SelltFirm, andBuytFirmby aggregating theNetSellt,Sellt, andBuytof all managers in a firm, respectively, and I take the average value ofSell/(Sell+Buy)tacross all managers within a firm to obtainSell/(Sell+Buy)tFirm. Lastly, I defineOptExertas the number of shares acquired through an option exercise (Execucomp item opt_exer_num) divided by the sum of the number of shares acquired through an option exercise and unexercised exercisable options (opt_unex_exer_num), andOptExertFirmas the average value ofOptExertacross all managers within a firm.
article.II. The Validity ofΔPOtas a Proxy for In:
In this section, I examine the two conditions that need to be satisfied to validate the use ofΔPOtas a proxy for information asymmetry–driven mispricing.
The results of the regression analysis are presented in TableII. The dependent variable isBHARt+1in all columns. The main variables of interest areΔPOtin columns (1) to (4) andΔPOtt+1andΔPOtrestin columns (5) to (8). Because a number of studies find evidence of postearnings announcement drift, that is, a tendency for a stock's cumulative abnormal returns to drift in the direction of an earnings surprise for several weeks following an earnings announcement, I control for earnings surprises in columns (3), (4), (7), and (8). Following Kinney, Burgstahler, and Martin (2002), I define earnings surprises (ESt) as actual earnings minus the last individual analyst forecast, scaled by price. In odd-numbered columns I control for firm and year fixed effects. To control for time-varying industry effects, I control for industry-year fixed effects in even-numbered columns.
Consistent with the view that predisclosed POs predict future stock prices, the coefficient onΔPOtis positive and statistically significant, as reported in columns (1) to (4). In addition, includingEStas a control variable does not reduce the statistical or economic significance of the coefficient onΔPOt. This result suggests thatΔPOtcontains information over and above earnings surprises. For example, the coefficient estimate shown in column (4) indicates that a one-standard-deviation increase inΔPOtis associated with an increase inBHARt+1of approximately 1.8 percentage points, which accounts for 5.1% of the standard deviation ofBHARt+1. The estimate also implies that an increase inΔPOtfrom the 25th percentile to the 75th percentile increasesBHARt+1by 0.3 percentage points.
When I separateΔPOtt+1andΔPOtrestin columns (5) to (8), the coefficient onΔPOtt+1is statistically and economically significant in all columns, whereas the coefficient onΔPOtrestis statistically significant at the 10% level in columns (5) and (7). This could reflect the fact that a significant portion ofPOtis concentrated inPOtt+1, with many firms not reportingPOtrest. The results shown in column (8) indicate that a one-standard-deviation increase inΔPOtt+1is associated with an increase inBHARt+1of approximately 2.7 percentage points, which accounts for approximately 7.7% of the standard deviation ofBHARt+1. The estimate also implies that an increase inΔPOtt+1from the 25th percentile to the 75th percentile increasesBHARt+1by 0.6 percentage points. The results in TableIIindicate thatΔPOtandΔPOtt+1are positively associated with stock returns duringt+1. As such, the first condition is satisfied.
To examine the second condition that predisclosed POs constitute private information I analyze stock price responses during a short time period around the disclosure of POs. If predisclosed POs are private information, stock prices should respond immediately to the disclosure of PO information. In contrast, if POs are public information before disclosure, stock prices should not respond significantly following disclosure.
The results of the analysis are presented in TableIII. The dependent variable isBHAR(−1,1) in all columns. The main variables of interest areΔPOtin columns (1) to (4) andΔPOtt+1andΔPOtrestin columns (5) to (8). I control for earnings surprises to address postearnings announcement drift in columns (3), (4), (7), and (8). I control for firm fixed effects in all columns, year fixed effects in odd-numbered columns, and industry-year fixed effects in even-numbered columns.
Consistent with the view that POs become public information following the disclosure of 10-Ks, the coefficients onΔPOtare all positive and statistically significant. Although the coefficient onEStis positive and statistically significant, as shown in columns (3) and (4), includingEStas a control variable does not change the statistical or economic significance of the coefficient onΔPOt. For example, the coefficient estimate shown in column (4) indicates that a one-standard-deviation increase inΔPOtis associated with an increase inBHAR(−1,1) of approximately 28 bps, which accounts for 7.6% of the standard deviation ofBHAR(−1,1). The estimate also implies that an increase inΔPOtfrom the 25th percentile to the 75th percentile increasesBHAR(−1,1) by 4 bps.
I separateΔPOtintoΔPOtt+1andΔPOtrestin columns (5) to (8). In all columns, onlyΔPOtt+1has a statistically and economically significant coefficient. The results shown in column (8) indicate that a one-standard-deviation increase inΔPOtt+1is associated with an increase inBHAR(−1,1) of approximately 33 bps, which accounts for 8.9% of the standard deviation ofBHAR(−1,1). The estimate also implies that an increase inΔPOtt+1from the 25th percentile to the 75th percentile increasesBHAR(−1,1) by 8 bps.11In contrast, none of the coefficients onΔPOtrestis positive or significant.
In sum, the results in this table support the view that predisclosed POs are private information. In addition, they help rule out an alternative explanation whereby the results presented in TableIIare driven by compensation for risk. The delayed price reactions suggest, however, that investors do not fully account for adverse selection on the part of managers when security issuance decisions are made. The overall results shown in TablesIIandIIIsuggest that predisclosed changes in POs are private and superior information that managers have about firm value.
article.III. The Effects of Changes in POs on Se:
In this section, I investigate the effects of information asymmetry–driven mispricing on security issuance usingΔPOtas a proxy for information asymmetry–driven mispricing.12
The regression results are reported in Panel A of TableIV. The dependent variable isNetEquityIssuetin columns (1) to (4). The variables of interest areΔPOtin columns (1) and (2) andΔPOtt+1andΔPOtrestin columns (3) and (4). The coefficient onΔPOtis negative and statistically significant, as reported in column (1). Because increases (decreases) in POs indicate undervaluation (overvaluation), this result is consistent with undervaluation (overvaluation) decreasing (increasing) contemporaneous net equity issuance. The coefficient estimate remains similar, as reported in column (2), after controlling for industry-year fixed effects. In particular, the coefficient of interest in column (2), −0.129, suggests that a one-standard-deviation decrease inΔPOtis associated with an increase in contemporaneous net equity issuance of 1.1% of total securities outstanding, which accounts for approximately 8.7% of the sample standard deviation ofNetEquityIssue. Another economic implication of this result is that a decrease inΔPOtfrom the 75th percentile to the 25th percentile is expected to increase equity issuance by 0.2% of total securities outstanding. The results shown in columns (3) and (4) suggest that this effect is due toΔPOtt+1. The coefficient estimate in column (4), −0.230, suggests that a one-standard-deviation decrease inΔPOtt+1is associated with an increase in contemporaneous net equity issuance of 1.8% of the value of total securities outstanding, which accounts for 13.8% of the sample standard deviation ofNetEquityIssue. The estimate also implies that a decrease inΔPOtt+1from the 75th percentile to the 25th percentile increases equity issuance by 0.4% of total securities outstanding. In contrast, the coefficient onΔPOtrestis insignificant. The coefficient onBHARtis positive and statistically significant at the 1% level in all columns. The coefficient onBHARt−1is positive and generally statistically significant in all columns. These results suggest that firms tend to issue equity following stock price run-ups.
In columns (5) to (8) the dependent variable isNetDebtIssuet. The coefficient onΔPOtis negative and statistically significant, as reported in columns (5) and (6). However, the economic magnitude of the coefficients is significantly smaller than those shown in columns (1) and (2). The coefficient of interest in column (6), −0.026, suggests that a one-standard-deviation decrease inΔPOtis associated with an increase in contemporaneous net debt issuance of 0.23% of total security outstanding, which accounts for approximately 2.3% of the sample standard deviation ofNetDebtIssue. The results shown in columns (7) and (8) suggest that these effects are due toΔPOtt+1. The coefficient estimate in column (8), −0.045, suggests that a one-standard-deviation decrease inΔPOtt+1is associated with an increase in contemporaneous net debt issuance of 0.35% of the value of total securities outstanding, which accounts for approximately 3.5% of the sample standard deviation of net debt issuance. Another economic implication of this result is that a decrease inΔPOtt+1from the 75th percentile to the 25th percentile is expected to increase debt issuance by 0.08% of total securities outstanding. As such, the effects of changes in POs are weaker on debt issuance than on equity issuance. These results are consistent with the price of debt being less sensitive to information than the price of equity. The coefficient onΔPOtrestis insignificant in both columns. Overall, the results in Panel A suggest that a manager's perception of mispricing strongly influences security issuance, especially equity issuance.
I next examine the association betweenΔPOtand the propensity to issue or reduce equity and debt. In particular, I run regressions in which the dependent variable isEquityIssueD,RepurchaseD,DebtIssueD, andDebtReductionD. The results are presented in Panel B. The dependent variable in columns (1) and (2) isEquityIssueDt. The coefficient onΔPOtin column (1), −0.348, is statistically significant at the 1% level. This coefficient estimate indicates that a one-standard-deviation decrease inΔPOtincreases the probability of equity issuance by 3.1 percentage points, which accounts for approximately 22.7% of the sample mean ofEquityIssueDt. The result also suggests that a decrease inΔPOtfrom the 75th percentile to the 25th percentile is expected to increase the probability of equity issuance by 0.4 percentage points. The results remain similar when I control for industry-year fixed effects in column (2). The coefficients onBHARtandBHARt−1are positive and statistically significant, suggesting that recent stock performance is positively associated with equity issuance. The dependent variable in columns (3) and (4) isRepurchaseDt. The coefficient onΔPOtis statistically significant and positive in both columns. The estimated coefficient reported in column (4), 0.304, indicates that a one-standard-deviation increase inΔPOtincreases the probability of equity repurchase by 2.7 percentage points, which accounts for approximately 14.6% of the sample mean ofRepurchaseDt. The estimate also implies that an increase inΔPOtt+1from the 25th percentile to the 75th percentile is expected to increase the probability of equity repurchases by 0.4 percentage points.
The dependent variable in columns (5) and (6) isDebtIssueDt. The coefficient onΔPOtis negative and statistically significant at the 5% level. The magnitudes of the coefficients, however, are approximately one-third of those shown in columns (1) and (2). In particular, the coefficient estimate in column (6) indicates that a one-standard-deviation decrease inΔPOtincreases the probability of debt issuance by 0.9 percentage points, which accounts for approximately 4.5% of the sample mean ofDebtIssueDt. The result also suggests that a decrease inΔPOtfrom the 75th percentile to the 25th percentile is expected to increase the probability of debt issuance by 0.1 percentage points. Finally, the dependent variable in columns (7) and (8) isDebtReductionDt. The coefficients onΔPOtare positive but statistically and economically insignificant, as reported in both columns. This result suggests that managers do not actively reduce outstanding debt when POs predict undervaluation of their firms. Thus, information asymmetry–driven mispricing affects debt issuance but not debt reductions, while on the equity side it affects both issues and repurchases of equity. In addition, the magnitude of the effects of information asymmetry–driven mispricing on security issues is significantly stronger for equity. These results are again consistent with the price of debt being less sensitive to information than the price of equity.
I next investigate the consequences of information asymmetry–driven mispricing for financial leverage. The results are presented in TableV. The dependent variable isLeveragetin all columns. The coefficient onΔPOtis significant and positive, as reported in column (1), indicating a positive association between changes in POs and contemporaneous leverage. To control for time-varying industry effects of leverage (Lemmon, Roberts, and Zender (2008)), I control for industry-year fixed effects in column (2). The coefficient of interest remains at a similar level. In columns (3) and (4), I separateΔPOtintoΔPOtt+1andΔPOtrest. The coefficients onΔPOtt+1andΔPOtrestare significant and positive, as reported in both columns. However, the coefficient onΔPOtt+1is statistically and economically more significant than that onΔPOtrest. Overall, the results in this table suggest that information asymmetry–driven undervaluation (overvaluation) has a positive (negative) effect on leverage, consistent with the results for security issuance in TableIV.
article.IV. The Level of Information Asymmetry a:
To test the validity of the interpretation that the negative association betweenΔPOtand security issuance is due to information asymmetry–driven mispricing, I conduct additional analyses in which I exploit variation in the level of information asymmetry. I first exploit cross-sectional variation in the level of information asymmetry and examine whether the association is stronger among firms with higher levels of information asymmetry. I then exploit time-varying information asymmetry and examine whether the timing of issuance is consistent with the information asymmetry–based explanation. I also exploit variation in levels of information-sensitivity between public debt and private debt, and examine whether issuance of information-sensitive debt is more negatively associatedΔPOt.
Here, I repeat theNetEquityIssueandNetDebtIssueanalyses after categorizing firms based on the level of information asymmetry. If information asymmetry–driven mispricing is behind my findings, the association betweenΔPOtandNetEquityIssuet(NetDebtIssuet) should be stronger among firms with high levels of information asymmetry. In contrast, if the association is driven by factors other than information-asymmetry problems, there is no clear reason for the association to be stronger among firms with high information asymmetry. I first exploit variation in the extent to which POs comprise private information. In particular, I separately analyze a small subset of sample firms that occasionally report POs on 10-Qs during the sample period. The level of information asymmetry due to predisclosed POs will be lower for these firms compared with firms that report POs only on annual reports. Next, I employ the number of analysts following, firm age, and R&D intensity to separate sample firms. Low (high) analyst following, young (old) firms, and high (low) R&D-intensity firms are categorized into high- (low-) information-asymmetry groups.
I report the results in TableVI. The dependent variable isNetEquityIssuetin Panel A. In column (1) I report the results of the regression in which I use firms that regularly report POs in their 10-Q filings during the sample period. Approximately 10% of sample firms occasionally report POs on 10-Qs even in the absence of material changes.13The coefficient onΔPOtis negative but statistically insignificant. In addition, the magnitude of the coefficient is less than half the estimated coefficient using all sample firms. These results indicate that the negative association betweenΔPOtandNetEquityIssuetdocumented in TableIVis weak, if it exists at all, among these firms, consistent with the view that the negative association betweenΔPOtandNetEquityIssuetis stronger among firms with high information asymmetry.
In columns (2) and (3) I report results of regressions using firms with high and low analyst following, respectively. I categorize a firm into the high-analyst-following group if its time-series average of analyst coverage at the firm level is larger than one and into the small-analyst-coverage group otherwise.14Although it is negative in both columns, the magnitude of the coefficient onΔPOtin column (4) is 63% larger than that in column (3). This result is consistent with the view that the effects of information asymmetry–driven mispricing on equity issuance are stronger among those firms that are associated with more severe information asymmetry problems.
In columns (4) and (5) I report results of theNetEquityIssueregression for old and young firms, respectively. To estimate a firm's age, I use the first year it appears in the Compustat database. The coefficient onΔPOtis negative, as reported in both columns. However, the absolute value of the reported coefficient is significantly larger and statistically more significant in column (5). This suggests that the negative association betweenΔPOtandNetEquityIssuetis more pronounced among young firms. In columns (6) and (7) I report results of theNetEquityIssueregression for firms with low and high R&D intensity, respectively, where R&D intensity is the time-series average of R&D expenditures over sales at the firm level. The coefficient onΔPOtis negative, as reported in both columns, but is economically and statistically significant only in column (7). This result suggests that the effects ofΔPOtare more pronounced among high-R&D-intensity firms. Overall, the results presented in Panel A are consistent with the view that the negative association betweenΔPOtandNetEquityIssuetis stronger among firms with more severe information-asymmetry problems, and thus support the interpretation that this association is due to information asymmetry.
The dependent variable isNetDebtIssuetin Panel B. In column (1) I report results of the regression for firms that regularly report POs in their 10-Q filings during the sample period. The coefficient onΔPOtis statistically insignificant, although the magnitude of the coefficient is larger than that shown in TableIV, where I use all firms in the regression. In columns (2) and (3) I report results of regressions using high and low analyst coverage, respectively. Although negative in both columns, the magnitude of the coefficient onΔPOtis larger in column (3), consistent with the effects of information asymmetry–driven mispricing on debt issuance being stronger among firms associated with more severe information asymmetry problems. In columns (4) and (5) I report results of theNetDebtIssueregression for old and young firms, respectively. The magnitude of the coefficient onΔPOtis larger in column (5) but the statistical significance is greater in column (4). Columns (6) and (7) present results for low- and high-R&D-intensity firms, respectively. The coefficient onΔPOtis negative, as reported in both columns, but is economically and statistically significant only in column (7), suggesting that the effects ofΔPOtare more pronounced among high-R&D-intensity firms. Overall, the results in Panel B are generally consistent with the negative association betweenΔPOtandNetDebtIssuetbeing stronger among firms with more severe information asymmetry problems. Nevertheless, the level of information asymmetry appears to play a more important role for equity issuance than debt issuance, which is again consistent with the view that the value of debt is less sensitive to information than the value of equity.
One would expect the level of information asymmetry regarding POs between managers and outside investors to increase as time passes following the most recent disclosure. If this were the case, the association betweenΔPOtand net security issuance should be stronger during the last fiscal quarter oft(Q4t). To test this prediction, I examine the association betweenΔPOtand net equity issuance (net debt issuance) during each fiscal quarter of yeart. The results of theNetEquityIssueanalyses are presented in Panel A of TableVII. The dependent variable is net equity issuance during Q1t, Q2t, Q3t, and Q4tin columns (1), (2), (3), and (4), respectively. The coefficient onΔPOtis negative and statistically significant in all four columns. However, the magnitude of the coefficient onΔPOtreported in column (4) is larger than those reported in columns (1) to (3). In addition, the coefficient onΔPOtreported in column (3) is larger than those reported in columns (1) and (2). As expected, these results are consistent with the negative association betweenΔPOtand net equity issuance being stronger as information asymmetry grows over time following the most recent 10-K filings.
I next examine whetherΔPOaffects equity issuance after the information asymmetry is resolved. In particular, I analyze the association betweenΔPOt−1andNetEquityIssueduring Q1t. BecauseΔPOt−1is no longer private information in Q1t, there should be no negative association betweenΔPOt−1andNetEquityIssueduring Q1tif the results in TableIVare due to information asymmetry. In contrast, the firm's fundamentals, including demand for external financing, likely remain similar from Q4t−1through Q1t. The results are presented in columns (5) and (6). The dependent variable is net equity issuance during Q1t, as reported in both columns. The variable of interest isΔPOt−1in column (5). As can be seen, the coefficient onΔPOt−1is neither economically nor statistically significant. In column (6), where I add bothΔPOt−1andΔPOtto the regression, only the coefficient onΔPOtis negative and statistically significant. This result suggests that the negative association betweenΔPOand equity issuance disappears as soon asΔPObecomes public information, in line with the information-asymmetry explanation.
In Panel B I repeat the analyses above forNetDebtIssue. The dependent variable is net debt issuance during Q1t, Q2t, Q3t, and Q4tin columns (1), (2), (3), and (4), respectively. The coefficient onΔPOtis negative, as reported in all four columns, but the magnitude and statistical significance of the coefficient onΔPOtis largest in column (4). The magnitude of the coefficient is approximately 2.5 times the sum of the coefficients of interest reported in columns (1) to (3), but the magnitude and statistical significance of the coefficients do not increase monotonically in columns (1) to (3). For example, the coefficient of interest is statistically significant in column (1) but not in columns (2) and (3), while the magnitude of the coefficient reported in column (1) is larger than those reported in columns (2) and (3).
In columns (5) and (6), I report results of analyses in which I examine whetherΔPOaffects debt issuance when it is no longer private information. The dependent variable is net debt issuance during Q1tin both columns. The coefficient onΔPOt−1reported in column (5) is positive but neither economically nor statistically significant. In column (6), the coefficient onΔPOt−1remains similar whenΔPOtis added to the regression and the coefficient onΔPOtis negative and statistically significant at the 10% level. These results suggest that the negative association betweenΔPOand debt issuance disappears whenΔPObecomes public information, again in line with the information asymmetry interpretation. Overall, the results in this table suggest that the negative association betweenΔPOand security issuance is stronger when information asymmetry is more severe, especially for equity issuance.
If information asymmetry drives the negative association betweenΔPOtand net debt issuance, the association should be stronger for public debt than for private debt. This is because public debt is more susceptible to information asymmetry problems than private debt, reflecting significant institutional differences between the two markets. For example, banks generally request information, both public and private, that they deem necessary to evaluate a borrower's financial health and then use that information to determine the terms of a contract. In addition, a borrower is typically required to provide periodic reports to a bank, which further reduces the costs associated with information asymmetry. Banks are also known to be better able to process information about and monitor borrowers than bondholders, who typically do not have access to information about or the ability to monitor borrowers. In addition, because public debt is typically held by dispersed arm's-length lenders, a free-rider problem arises in monitoring.
To test whether the negative association withΔPOtis stronger for public debt than for private debt, I take advantage of the Capital IQ database and separately examine public debt issues and private debt issues. In particular, I definePublicDebtIssueas bonds and notes minus lagged bonds and notes, scaled by the lagged value of total securities, in which bonds and notes are computed as the sum of senior bonds and notes and subordinated bonds and notes. I definePrivateDebtIssueas nonpublic debt minus lagged nonpublic debt, scaled by the lagged value of total securities.
TableVIIIreports the results. I restrict the sample to firm-year observations covered by the Capital IQ database. I first check whether the results of regressions ofNetEquityIssueandNetDebtIssuein TableIVhold in this sample. The dependent variables areNetEquityIssueandNetDebtIssuein columns (1) and (2), respectively. In both columns, the statistical and economic significance of the coefficient onΔPOtremain similar to those shown in TableIV. The dependent variable isPublicDebtIssuein column (3). The coefficient onΔPOtis negative and statistically significant. Moreover, at −0.069, the coefficient is more than twice that reported in column (2). The dependent variable isPrivateDebtIssuein column (4). The coefficient onΔPOtis neither statistically nor economically significant. Moreover, the sign on the coefficient is not negative. These results suggest that the negative association betweenΔPOtand net debt issuance is due to public debt issuance, in line with the interpretation that the negative association betweenΔPOand contemporaneous net debt issuance is due to information asymmetry–driven mispricing.
article.V. Alternative Explanations:
In this section, I address alternative explanations. First, I consider the concern that the negative association betweenΔPOtand contemporaneous security issues may be due to time-varying demand for external financing that is correlated with changes in POs. To this end, I investigate whetherΔPOtis associated with a contemporaneous or future financing deficit. I next address the possibility that inactive equity activities, such as scheduled employee stock grants, are driving the negative association betweenΔPOtandNetEquityIssuet.
Here I consider the possibility that the negative association betweenΔPOtand security issues that I document is due to time-varying fundamentals that are correlated with changes in POs. Although recent changes in fundamentals of which the market is aware will be captured in contemporaneous and one-year-lagged abnormal stock returns,ΔPOtcould capture information about demand for external financing that is over and above what the market knows. For example, a decrease (increase) in PO may indicate lower (higher) cash flows in the following year and thus a firm's need for external financing may increase (decrease). To address this possibility, I examine whetherΔPOtis associated with a contemporaneous or future financing deficit. To this end, I follow Frank and Goyal (2003) and defineDEFtas cash dividends plus investment plus the change in working capital minus internal cash flow, scaled by lagged assets.
The results are presented in TableIX. I first examine the association between changes in POs and a contemporaneous financing deficit. The dependent variable isDEFtand the variable of interest isΔPOtin columns (1) and (2). In column (1), I do not control for firm characteristics. The coefficient of interest is neither statistically nor economically significant. I control for firm characteristics in column (2) but the coefficient of interest remains qualitatively unchanged, suggesting thatΔPOtis not significantly associated with a contemporaneous financing deficit. In columns (3) and (4), I report results on whether changes in POs are associated with future financing deficits. The dependent variable isDEFt+1in both columns. Again, none of the coefficients of interest is statistically significant, indicating that changes in POs are not associated with future financing deficits. Overall, the results reported in this table mitigate the concern thatΔPOis negatively associated with demand for external financing and could drive the results presented in TableIV.
To mitigate the concern that inactive equity activities, such as scheduled employee stock grants, that are included inNetEquityIssuetare driving the results, I use alternative measures of net equity issuance. In particular, I defineSEOminusREPOSDCas the difference between the dollar value of SEOs and stock repurchases, both of which come from the SDC database, scaled by lagged total securities outstanding. Because Banyi, Dyl, and Kahle (2008) find that publicly announced share repurchase programs are often not reported by SDC, and the Compustat measures provide fairly accurate estimates of actual total share repurchases for firms with SDC coverage, I defineSEOminusREPOCpstalternatively as SEOs minus PRSTKC in Compustat, scaled by lagged total securities outstanding. I also defineSEODas an indicator variable that is equal to one if a firm engages in an SEO in a given year and zero otherwise.
The results are presented in TableX. The dependent variable isSEOminusREPOtSDCin columns (1) and (2). In column (1), I report results of a regression in which I use all sample firms. The coefficient onΔPOtis negative and statistically significant, although its magnitude is smaller than that reported in TableIV. Because only 6% of firm-year observations are associated with SEOs and a number of sample firms never appear in the SDC database,15I next repeat the analyses after excluding firms that report no SEOs during the sample period. The results are presented in column (2). The magnitude of the coefficient onΔPOtincreases significantly. The coefficient estimate suggests that a one-standard-deviation decrease inΔPOtis associated with an increase inSEOminusREPOtSDCof approximately 0.8% of total securities outstanding, which accounts for approximately 10.4% of the sample standard deviation ofSEOminusREPOtSDC. In columns (3) and (4), I report results of the regressions ofSEOminusREPOtCpst. The magnitudes of the coefficients are slightly larger than those presented in columns (1) and (2). The coefficient estimates shown in column (4) suggest that a one-standard-deviation decrease inΔPOtis associated with an increase inSEOminusREPOtCpstof approximately 0.9% of total securities outstanding, which accounts for approximately 11.0% of the sample standard deviation ofSEOminusREPOtSDC. In columns (5) and (6) I report results of the regressions ofSEODt. When I include all sample firms in the analysis, the coefficient onΔPOtis -0.152 and statistically significant. When I exclude firms that never engage in SEOs during the sample period according to SDC, the coefficient onΔPOtis −0.435 and statistically significant. Overall, the results in this table indicate that the negative association betweenΔPOtand contemporaneous equity issuance remains strong for measures of equity issuance that are not affected by inactive equity activities and thus suggest that inactive equity activities are not driving the negative association betweenNetEquityIssuetandΔPOt.
article.VI. Do Managers Believe thatΔPOtPredicts:
In this section, I examine whether managers appear to believe thatΔPOtpredicts mispricing. To do so, I first examine managers' private trading activities. If managers believe thatΔPOtpredicts mispricing, they will sell their firms' shares whenΔPOtpredicts overvaluation and purchase shares whenΔPOtpredicts undervaluation. In addition, I analyze the association betweenΔPOtand contemporaneous option exercises on the part of managers. Because, according to Ofek and Yermack (2000), nearly all executive stock option exercises are followed by share sales, managers will exercise options whenΔPOtpredicts overvaluation.
The results of the analyses of managers' trading activities are reported in TableXI. In Panel A, I conduct the analyses at the manager level. In these analyses, I construct a panel of managers for each firm. In particular, I define the first (last) year a given manager reports sales or purchases of equity as the first (last) year the manager works for the firm. If no trading is reported in a given year, I set the number of shares sold and the number of shares purchased to zero. In odd-numbered columns I control forBHARtandBHARt−1as well as for manager and year fixed effects. In even-numbered columns I additionally control for firm characteristics. For columns (1) and (2) the dependent variable isNetSellt. The coefficient onΔPOtis negative and statistically significant at the 1% level in both columns, suggesting that managers engage in significantly more stock sales (purchases) when predisclosed POs predict overvaluation (undervaluation) of shares. The coefficient estimate in column (1) indicates that, for an average manager in the sample, a one-standard-deviation decrease inΔPOtis associated with an increase in net share sales of approximately 4.6%.
I next investigate whether the results in columns (1) and (2) are due to sales or purchases of shares. To this end, I run the regressions after separating sales and purchases of shares. In columns (3) and (4) the dependent variable isSellt. The coefficient onΔPOtis negative and statistically significant. In addition, the magnitudes of the coefficient estimates are similar to those in columns (1) and (2). These results suggest that the effects ofΔPOtonNetSelltmay be driven mostly by sales of shares. The coefficient estimate in column (3) indicates that, for an average manager in the sample, a one-standard-deviation decrease inΔPOtis associated with an increase in share sales of approximately 4.0%. In columns (5) and (6) the dependent variable isBuyt. Although the magnitudes of the coefficients onΔPOtare approximately one-tenth the size of those in columns (3) and (4), they are as statistically and economically significant as those in columns (3) and (4). For example, the coefficient estimate in column (5) indicates that, for an average manager in the sample, a one-standard-deviation increase inΔPOtis associated with an increase in share purchases of approximately 6.2%. The small coefficients are consistent with the fact that transactions executed by managers are predominantly sales. In columns (7) and (8) the dependent variable isSell/(Sell+Buy)t. The coefficient onΔPOtis negative and statistically significant in both columns. The pattern of managers' trading activities therefore suggests that managers believe thatΔPOtpredicts mispricing. The coefficients onBHARtandBHARt−1are positive and statistically significant when the dependent variable isNetSellt,Sellt, orSell/(Sell+Buy)t. This result is consistent with prior studies. For example, Seyhun (1992) shows that insiders are more likely to sell shares following periods of price appreciation, and Piotroski and Roulstone (2005) find that insider trading is inversely related to recent returns.
I next analyze managers' trading activities in aggregate at the firm level. The results are presented in Panel B. The dependent variables areNetSelltFirm,SelltFirm,BuytFirm, andSell/(Sell+Buy)tFirmin columns (1) and (2), (3) and (4), (5) and (6), and (7) and (8), respectively. The interpretation of the coefficients onΔPOtis similar to that in Panel A in all columns. Overall, the results reported in this table are consistent with the view that managers believe thatΔPOtpredicts mispricing and use such information in private trading.
In TableXII, I report results of option-exercise analyses. In columns (1) and (2), I report results on the option exercises of CEOs. In columns (3) and (4), I report results on all executives' option exercises. In columns (1) to (4), the dependent variable isOptExert. In these tests, I control for manager fixed effects and standard errors are clustered at the manager level. The coefficient estimate onΔPOtis consistently negative and statistically significant in columns (1) to (4). For example, the coefficient estimate in column (1) indicates that a one-standard-deviation decrease inΔPOtis associated with an increase in CEOs'OptExertof approximately 1.9 percentage points, which accounts for 8.1% of the standard deviation and 14.3% of the mean value of CEOs'OptExert. The coefficients onBHARtandBHARt−1are positive and statistically significant in all columns, suggesting that option exercises are active following periods of price appreciation. In columns (5) and (6), I report results on option exercises at the firm level. The dependent variable isOptExertFirm. In these columns, I control for firm fixed effects and standard errors are clustered at the firm level. The coefficient onΔPOtis negative and statistically significant in both columns, suggesting that in aggregate managers engage in more option exercises whenΔPOtpredicts overvaluation of firm shares. Overall, the results in this table are consistent with managers believing thatΔPOtpredicts mispricing.
article.VII. Summary and Conclusions:
This paper provides empirical evidence on the effects of information asymmetry–driven mispricing on security issuance. By exploiting changes in predisclosure POs as a proxy for information asymmetry–driven mispricing, I show that firms avoid (prefer) issuing equity and debt when they are undervalued (overvalued). However, the effects of information asymmetry–driven mispricing are significantly stronger for net equity issuance than net debt issuance. Consequently, undervaluation leads to increased leverage. In line with the interpretation that these results are due to information asymmetry problems, the effects are stronger for firms, periods, and securities associated with high information asymmetry. Managers' private trading and option exercises suggest that managers believe that changes in POs predict mispricing.
This paper contributes to studies on market timing. Unlike most studies on market timing, I capture mispricing by exploiting managerial information that is unknown to the public. This renders the results less open to alternative explanations. In addition, the measure makes it possible to distinguish information asymmetry–driven mispricing from investor irrationality-driven mispricing. This paper is also related to studies on the effects of information asymmetry on capital structure. Unlike most such studies, however, I consider the sign of mispricing caused by information asymmetry in addition to the magnitude of information asymmetry. I show that the sign of mispricing plays an important role in security issuance decisions. More importantly, the results in this paper suggest that disclosing POs and other variables associated with managers' perceptions of future firm performance on annual and quarterly filings would help mitigate information asymmetry problems between insiders and outsiders.
Editors: Stefan Nagel, Philip Bond, Amit Seru, and Wei Xiong
article.Appendix B: Alternative Measures ofΔPOt:
A potential concern arising from the wayΔPOtis defined is that changes in POs could have been expected by investors, based on available information from yeart−1. For example, investors would expectΔPOtto be positive if firm characteristics as oft−1 predict thatPOtwill be larger thanPOt−1. Although the results presented in TableIIIsuggest thatΔPOtis news to investors around 10-K issuance, I address this concern by using unexpected changes in POs. In particular, I estimate an expected value ofPOt(E[POt]) using POs of the previous year(s) and firm characteristics as oft−1. I then defineΔPÔtasPOtminus E[POt].
TableBIreports the results. In column (1) I present results of a regression ofPOtonPOt−1and firm characteristics as oft−1. The only coefficient that is statistically and economically significant isPOt−1, suggesting that other publicly available information does not predictPOt. Nevertheless, I use the results presented in column (1) to predict E[POt], computeΔPÔt, and run regressions of security issuance. The results of the security issuance regressions are reported in columns (2) and (3). The dependent variable isNetEquityIssuetin column (2). The coefficient estimate onΔPÔtis negative and significant at the 10% level. The dependent variable isNetDebtIssuetin column (3). The coefficient onΔPÔtis negative but statistically insignificant.
I next addPOt−2to the information set for predictingPOt. The results of the regression ofPOtare presented in column (4). The coefficient onPOt−1remains similar to that in column (1). In contrast, the estimated coefficient onPOt−2is neither statistically nor economically significant. The coefficients on other control variables generally remain similar to those shown in column (1). The results of the regression ofNetEquityIssuetare reported in column (5). The economic and statistical significance of the coefficient onΔPÔtincrease slightly relative to those reported in column (2). In contrast, the coefficient onΔPÔtis statistically insignificant, as reported in column (6), for which the dependent variable isNetDebtIssuet.
